data.title: ImmunityBio rises after EU approval of bladder cancer therapy
data.description:
• ImmunityBio (IBRX) traded higher on Wednesday after the European Commission issued a conditional marketing authorization for its bladder cancer therapy, Anktiva, expanding its use in more than two dozen EU countries.
  • The decision came after an expert panel of the EU drug regulator, the European Medicines Agency, endorsed the immunotherapy in December as part of a combination regimen for a type of invasive bladder cancer.
  • Specifically, the authorization allows the company to market Anktiva across 27 countries in combination with Bacillus Calmette-Guérin ((BCG)) for adults with BCG-unresponsive non-muscle invasive bladder cancer.
  • As part of the condition approval, which was based on IBRX’s Phase 2/3 QUILT-3.032 study, ImmunityBio (IBRX) is required to provide EU regulators with long-term safety and efficacy data related to Anktiva.

data.title: Liberty Global reports Q4 results
data.description:
• Liberty Global press release (LBTYK): Q4 Adjusted EBITDA of £965.4 million, -2.4% YoY on a reported and rebased basis.
  • Revenue of $1.23B (+9.8% Y/Y) beats by $30M.
  • Broadband net losses of 16,700, a sequential improvement despite continued intense competition
  • Postpaid net losses of 164,800, primarily driven by elevated churn during the 30-day exit window following the October price rise announcement
  • Fixed ARPU declined by 0.8% YoY due to pricing pressure, in particular during the Black Friday period

data.title: Starbucks faces shareholder pressure over its labor battles
data.description:
A group of Starbucks (SBUX) shareholders is pressuring the coffee chain giant's board over what it said are sustained oversight failures of labor relations, according to Business Insider.

The group of long-term investors is asking other shareholders to vote against the reelection of Jørgen Vig Knudstorp, the lead independent director, and Beth Ford, the chair of the nominating and corporate governance committee.

Seattle-based Starbucks (SBUX) is still embroiled in a labor dispute with the Starbucks Workers United union. Contract negotiations that began in early 2024 have stalled, with the union demanding higher pay (a $20/hour base with 5% annual raises), better staffing, more consistent schedules, and resolution of hundreds of unfair labor practice charges. In November, workers launched the "Red Cup Rebellion" strike, which expanded to over 670 stores and 12,000 workers as of early 2026. Workers have filed over 125 new unfair labor practice charges since January 2025, and Starbucks paid a record $38.9 million settlement to New York City over workweek law violations. 

"We have a world-class board of directors with deep, relevant expertise, who are supporting our management team in driving a business turnaround," stated a Starbucks spokesperson on the developments. The company also continues to defend its workplace policies, employee benefits, and wage structure.

Shares of Starbucks (SBUX) are up more than 13% on a year-to-date basis.

data.title: Wall Street pushes higher as tech regains momentum
data.description:
Wall Street’s major market averages pushed higher on Wednesday as the tech sector rebounds and regains momentum.

On the trading session, the tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was last up by +0.3%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) added +0.3%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was up +0.2%.

From a sector vantage point, seven of the S&P 500’s 11 segments were in positive trading territory, with energy leading the charge. At the other end of the spectrum, utilities has suffered the most so far.

Treasury yields inched higher as the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) moved up 2 basis points to 3.46%, and the longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) moved up by 2 basis points to 4.08%. 

On the economic front, durable goods orders fell less than expected in December, while housing starts and building permits rose in the same month. 

Additionally, industrial production figures increased more than expected in January and the FOMC minutes will be released later in the day. 

As for stocks that were on the move, shares of Garmin (GRMN) climbed 17.5%, and shares of Palo Alto Networks (PANW) came down by 8.5%.

data.title: Record bond trading as tech borrowing, M&A drive supply wave - report
data.description:
Investors are reportedly turning to the secondary market to buy debt, as demand for corporate bonds has left companies unable to issue new securities at the same rate.

The demand is also leading to record trading volumes, with more than $61 billion in U.S. corporate bonds traded per day on average in January, Bloomberg reported, citing data from Crisil Coalition Greenwich. The number is 11% higher than the same period last year. 

The rise in demand also comes from quantitative traders that are growing more active in corporate bonds as geopolitical tensions weigh on sentiments. 

Quantitative traders are growing increasingly active in corporate bonds, boosting liquidity in many of the securities. And geopolitical tensions around Greenland and Venezuela have given investors more reason to cut exposure to some companies and boost holdings in others.

Big technology “hyperscalers” are expected to borrow as much as $400 billion this year to finance artificial intelligence spending, while companies are also issuing debt to fund a sharp rebound in mergers and acquisitions. U.S. M&A volume has jumped 181% year-to-date to more than $414 billion, from about $147 billion during the same period last year, according to data cited by Bloomberg.

Large transactions are drawing strong demand. Oracle’s (ORCL) $25 billion bond sale generated more than $129 billion in orders—the largest book of its kind—and was followed by heavy trading in its outstanding securities. Bloomberg said active secondary markets are helping compress risk premiums and enabling investors to rebalance portfolios into newer issues.

Average daily trading in investment-grade and high-yield bonds reached $50 billion in 2025, up from $46 billion in 2024 and the highest level on record, Bloomberg reported. Automation has boosted volumes by more than 40% since 2022, even as sell-side trading desk headcount has declined.

U.S. high-grade bond spreads averaged 0.79 percentage points on Friday, near multi-decade lows. While yields remain attractive and continue to support inflows, Bloomberg noted that heavy issuance and looser covenants could leave weaker credits more vulnerable as dispersion becomes a defining theme heading into 2026.

Fixed Income ETFs: (TLT), (TLH), (IEF), (IEI), (SHY), (SGOV), (SCHO), (BIL), (AGG), (BND), (VCIT), (MUB), (MBB), (JNK), (LQD), (HYG), (VTIP), (TIP), (SCHP), (STIP), (TIPX), (SPIP), (WIP), (GTIP), (LQDI), and (RINF).

data.title: Moderna dealt blows in ongoing patent dispute with Arbutus
data.description:
A federal judge in Delaware has thrown out two key defenses Moderna (MRNA) had been claiming in its ongoing patent litigation with Arbutus Biopharma (ABUS) regarding its mRNA COVID-19 vaccine.

Judge Joshua Wolson tossed Moderna's first defense based on obviousness. "All of the obviousness challenges that Moderna raises in this case are ones that it raised or could have raised in the [inter partes review], a fact that Moderna doesn't dispute," Wolson wrote in a memorandum. "It therefore cannot assert those obviousness claims in this case."

He added that IPR estoppel prevents a defendant from raising obviousness claims made in an IPR proceeding, which Moderna already did and lost.

Moderna's other argument, issue preclusion, argues that the subject matter claimed in a patent was not actually invented by Arbutus. "Moderna failed to demonstrate that there was a prior conception of Arbutus's invention by another," Wolson wrote. "Moderna’s own expert, Dr. Prud’homme, conceded that an Arbutus scientist, Stephen Reid, encapsulated mRNA in lipid-nanoparticles years before Moderna encapsulated mRNA."

One bright spot for Moderna is that Wolson is allowing its enablement defense to proceed. "Enablement serves the dual function of ensuring adequate disclosure of the claimed invention and of preventing claims broader than the disclosed invention," according to the US Patent and Trademark Office.

data.title: Investor fears are ‘probably exaggerated' – HSBC’s Max Kettner
data.description:
Investor fears surrounding big tech and hyperscaler companies are “probably exaggerated,” according to Max Kettner, chief multi asset strategist at HSBC, who points to the sector’s continued profitability as evidence that the market selloff has gone too far.

Kettner noted that the top 10 stocks by market cap in the S&P 500 (SP500)—Nvidia (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet Class A and C (GOOGL), (GOOG), Meta (META), Broadcom (AVGO), Tesla (TSLA), and Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B)—have a combined return on equity of approximately 65%, up dramatically from just 15% in 2017-2018.

“We’re still talking about companies that make really a lot of money,” he said in an interview with CNBC.

The strategist highlighted striking valuation anomalies that suggest the market has overcorrected.

Amazon (AMZN), for instance, now trades at a price-to-earnings ratio near its 20-year low, comparable to levels seen during the depths of the 2008 financial crisis.

The “Max Seven” hyperscalers (MAGS) trade at a PE of around 26, while the Russell Index (IWM) trades at nearly 25 despite almost half of its companies posting negative earnings.

“There’s a lot of stuff within that rotation over the last four, five months, a little bit of underperformance we’ve seen within the Mag 7 that made them really look incredibly cheap now,” Kettner explained.

HSBC is now advocating a shift away from high-beta momentum stocks and back toward the Mag 7 (MAGS), combined with a focus on sectors tied to the lower end of the K-shaped economy.

This “two-sided approach” targets homebuilders (XHB), regional banks (KRE), (IAT), transport companies (IYT), (XTN), and U.S. retail (XRT), (RTH)—sectors positioned to benefit from tax reimbursements expected in February and April.

Kettner emphasized that economists have consistently underestimated the resilience of the U.S. consumer, noting that actual consumption has exceeded expectations every quarter since 2023.

“I do really think that consensus once again is underestimating the sheer depth and just sheer size of tax reimbursements and tax refunds now coming in February and in April,” he said.

For investors seeking safe-haven assets, Kettner recommended the Swiss franc (FXF) over the U.S. dollar (DXY), citing unstable correlations between the dollar and risk assets since Liberation Day.

Despite his bullish outlook on tech and cyclicals, Kettner acknowledged risks, including the possibility of monthly inflation spikes that could trigger a “2022-style sell-off” where higher yields prompt losses across asset classes. This risk, he said, temporarily constrains the safe-haven characteristics of Treasuries.

Artificial Intelligence/Robotics ETFs: (AIQ), (BOTZ), (DTEC), (WTAI), (XAIX), (WISE), (GINN), (ROBT), (TECB), (XT), (THNQ), and (CHAT).

Semiconductor ETFs: (SMH), (SOXX), (SOXL), (FTXL), (XSD), (USD), (PSI), and (SEMI).

Cloud Computing ETFs: (SKYY), (WCLD), (CLOU), (FCLD), (SKYU), and (CLOD).

Mag 7 Focused ETFs: (MAGS), (MAGX), (QQQU), and (QQQD).

data.title: Palo Alto's recent acquisitions improve AI positioning but also drag on near-term EPS: analysts
data.description:
Palo Alto Networks (PANW) shares were down 8% during early trading on Wednesday following its second-quarter results and guidance, but most financial firms remain positive on growth drivers and recommend buying on any weakness.

The cybersecurity company readily surpassed second-quarter expectations and increased its full-year revenue guidance by nearly $800M to a midpoint of $11.295B, which obliterated the $10.53B consensus estimate. However, it also lowered its adjusted earnings per share total for the year to a midpoint of $3.675 versus its prior midpoint of $3.85.

Wedbush Securities said the reduced EPS was related to its recent acquisitions of Chronosphere and CyberArk, which led to a sizable increase in shares. The firm reiterated its Outperform rating and $225 price target, adding, "We would be strong buyers in knee-jerk weakness of shares tomorrow at the open."

Meanwhile, Morgan Stanley retained its Overweight rating but reduced its price target to $223 from $245.

"With FQ2 results, the company was able to give guidance for CyberArk and Chronosphere impacts, removing an overhang that has been in the stock since last summer," said Morgan Stanley analysts, led by Meta Marshall, in a Wednesday investor note. "Many investors we talked to had wanted to get past the reset, particularly on CyberArk, to feel comfortable stepping into the name. While we still need more evidence of CyberArk's trajectory and synergy realization, we at least have baselines to measure against with the $200mm ARR starting point for Chronosphere in FQ2 and the >$1.2bn ARR for CyberArk for FQ3."

Needham reiterated its Buy rating but also lowered its price target to $200 from $230.

"Management's updated 3QFY26 & FY26 outlook was in line with consensus estimates for NGS ARR, RPO, and Revenue on an organic basis, while Operating Margin, EPS, and Free Cash Flow Margin were lowered to account for the carrying cost of the Chronosphere and CyberArk acquisitions," said Needham analysts, led by Mike Cikos, in a note. "While management did not specify the acquired cost base, the company reaffirmed an Adjusted Free Cash Flow Target of 40% in FY28, with 37% in FY26 & FY27, and FY30 NGS ARR of $20 Billion. Palo Alto has already set CyberArk integration plans in motion, supporting management commentary for a swift integration timeline."

Jefferies also retained its Buy rating but reduced its price target to $215 from $250.

"CYBR's CY25 FCF margin was only 19% (23% adjusted), meaning that PANW has to approximately double this to reach its target of 40% FCF margins for the combined business," said Jefferies analysts, led by Joseph Gallo, in a note. "PANW also expects 37% adj. FCF margins in FY27 and reaffirmed its previous target of 40% in FY28. Cost synergies, including lower cloud hosting costs, close to double-digit percentage job cuts for the CYBR business, and other efficiencies, should help it reach its target."

Koi acquisition to boost agentic AI capabilities

Before releasing its earnings, Palo Alto announced it was acquiring the Israel-based Koi. Palo Alto CEO Nikesh Arora discussed the acquisition during the earnings call but did not provide financial details of the deal. Some reports indicated the acquisition costs approximately $400M.

"We are witnessing a dramatic shift in how software now lives on the endpoint; traditional security tools are often blind to the new AI layer of software," Arora said. "The massive rise of MCP servers, browser extensions, plug-ins, and ephemeral code that bypasses standard security controls. This represents a significant unmanaged attack surface."

"We identified this new threat vector early, and Palo Alto Networks has been a customer of Koi since the summer of 2025," he added. "On my recent trip to Israel in December, Lee Klarich and I met with the Koi team and were immediately impressed by their foresight into the next generation of endpoint threats. Since then, we've seen the risk pattern intensify, including security concerns that have been recently popularized by the widespread adoption of open cloud. We believe this is the latest example of what the future of an AI attack surface will look like, and Koi will help our XDR platform remain well positioned to provide the most innovative security solutions to our customers."

Other cybersecurity firms were mostly down during early Wednesday trading as well. CrowdStrike (CRWD) and Zscaler (ZS) had both declined 2%, while SentinelOne (S) and Fortinet (FTNT) were both down 1%.
